sentences.text: Before I hooked up with my friend Oscar, I remember an incident which was of some importance, because it catalogs my first encounter with Jewish women after the liberation.
On the 8th of May, Germany formally surrendered.
And General Eisenhower had proclamations in German and in English on all the buildings in the cities and villages.
We went to the town of Eggenfelden to read those proclamations.
And somebody mentioned, in the course of conversation with some of the survivors, that he heard that in the village of Simbach, somebody saw some Jewish women.
That night when we returned back to the farm where we were staying, the conversation revolved around the Jewish women.
As I said, the hope against hope was maybe, maybe these women are women that our kin, our relatives, or maybe they know they were at camp.
And other woman, maybe they know where our relatives are.
And the decision was made-- despite the fact that we were physically run down-- that we will go and try to find out what is to be found out.

So myself and two other fellows, younger fellows, we got together many of the things that the black GIs dropped off-- from the K-rations, C-rations, cigarettes, canned food, SPAM, God forbid, and whatever there was, cheese.
And we took with us.
And we got a lift from an American, from two American soldiers that were going in the Jeep in that direction.
They dropped us off about 10 miles.
They gave us a lift, and we went on foot.
And then we got very tired, so we hired a German farmer.
And we gave him all our cigarettes for taking us to the town of Simbach.
When we arrived in Simbach, we asked, are there any women inmates?
Because we all wore this, so this is-- and then we were directed to a farmhouse.
We came to the farmhouse.
And there were three other prisoners like us from another convoy that came there for the same reason.
They heard the same story that there are Jewish women there.
And we were exchanging some information.
It was a barren room.
You could tell that the there was some pictures that the women-- that you could see the white mark, where the crucifix hung, that they took off the wall.
And after a while, these three women came in.
We have seen each other in terrible shapes.
We saw friends dying next to us.
We could tell when the person was going to die, how many weeks he had to live.
We could tell.
If your feet swell up, you had four weeks to live.
And after the swelling went down, you got two weeks.
And with our friends, you go to the latrine, we used to follow.
Because we knew today or tomorrow he's going to die.
And we used to watch them sometimes die sitting on the latrine.
We knew, we could tell.
We had [GERMAN], people who were walking skeletons.
But we were not prepared for what we have seen.
We have never seen Jewish women emaciated, drawn faces, their heads shaven, with the long caftans.
And we started-- of course they were not our kin.
We started the conversation.
Two of them were from Slovakia.
One of them was from [SLOVAK].
They knew nothing about our relatives.
They were in Auschwitz, and then they were in the transport.
And they went to a factory, and then they were-- the war ended when they were on the march.
And we gave them all the food that we brought with us, everything that we had.
And at one point, the woman got up and they said, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it's time to light Shabbos candle-- [SOBBING] it was time to light Shabbos candles.
We had no idea.
I mean, for us, Shabbos didn't mean anything anymore.
We didn't even know that it was a Friday night.
And yet these women, with kerchiefs on their head, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] lit the candles.
And the six of us, the men slowly broke out into the Shabbos song of welcome, Shalom aleichum, and we linked arms.
And somehow, we felt that we were victorious, that we defied the designs of the Nazis.
And we survived, not only in flesh [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but in spirit.
Strangely as it may seem, there was hope.
Because we looked at Jewish women, and we knew there were going be Jewish home, there were going to be Jewish children.
And we were not vanquished.
It took a long time afterwards before we saw another Jewish woman.
And shortly after this, I joined my friend Oscar and went to work for the American Army.
As I told you, it was the 35th Tank Battalion.
The captain on our unit was a wonderful man, Captain Kingsley.
He was a teacher from Buffalo.
And when we became so taken with stories about American life that the GIs used to tell us, we made the decision, both Oscar and I, that we want to go to the United States.
When he heard that, he said, well, if you want to go the States, why don't you get an education.
There's nothing that will help you as much as an education.
You know the extent of my education.
He said, why don't to go to college?
I said, how can I go to college?
I have eight grades.
OK, I read a lot.
I used to do homework with my cousins who went to high school.
But I was not college-bound.
He engaged a Russian refugee, Professor [?
Stahofsky, ?] who was later on the director of UNRRA hospital at Regensburg.
He was a ear and throat man.
He was also a linguist.
He spoke many languages.
So did his wife.
His daughter, his little daughter, four-year-old daughter, spoke four languages.
And this man prepared us for college entrance exam.
I took six years of Latin in six months, algebra, physics, whatever we thought was necessary for college entrance exams.
And we tried to get to the Marburg University, we couldn't get in.
We eventually were admitted to Johann Wolfgang Goethe University in Frankfurt am Main.
My friend Oscar wanted to study medicine.
I had no intention to cure mankind.
I wanted to cure myself and I wanted to take philosophy.
I wanted to find out what made people tick.
So while we worked for the American Army, we studied at night.
And then when the company-- we returned the armor to [GERMAN].
And most of the guys were leaving on points to the United States.
And the company moved out.
And we finished working for them in June of 1946, in the fall of 1946.
We entered the university at Frankfurt.
My friend could not get into the medical faculty right away.
He was advised to take anything, could stay as a student.
And after one semester he would have priority.
So that's what we did.
And I stayed.
I took philosophy and languages.
We had the Jewish student organization that had over 100 Jewish students in the city of Frankfurt, at the University of Frankfurt.
In 1948, I was elected president of that organization.
And I worked hard to make sure that even after I immigrated to the States, there was enough money to put all the students who had to go through the school program.
I accomplished this by running some benefit dances, by getting in touch, becoming sponsored by the Hillel organization of the University of Chicago.
And they used to send us clothing.
Later on, I persuaded them not to send clothing but to send coffee beans we could sell on the black market and finance our expenses with that.
In 1950, in my fourth year of my studies, I was working on my PhD, when the council called me for the third time.
And he said Norbert, if you don't leave now to the United States, I'll have to cancel your visa.
I registered for immigration in the United States in 1946.
In end of 1948, I was called in.
I said, I wanted to finish my studies.
I prolonged for six months, another six months.
Finally, council said, you go now, or you can't go at all.
So I said, to hell with the PhD in the German university.
And I did put my books and one set of suits in the foot locker.
And I went to Bremen, and I boarded the General WG Haan, which was a liberty ship used to transport the GIs in World War II.
And I went to United States.
I went to New Orleans, because supposedly I had a work contract in the city of Atlanta.
And I arrived in New Orleans on June 5, which was a Sunday.
On Monday, June 6, which is in non-Jewish calendar, the secular Gregorian calendar, St.
Norbert's Day.
On June 6, I stepped down on American soil in New Orleans.
On June 7, which is today, I arrived in Atlanta.
There was another girl student from Marburg University.
And two young woman from the HIAS waited for us at the station, the Atlanta railroad station.
And they tried very hard in broken Yiddish to tell us who they are.
And we told them, please do not bother yourself.
We both speak English.
You can speak English.
They were so elated that they took us out for lunch.
Although I wound up paying for lunch, because I wasn't about to have as the first thing happening to me in the United States of America, a woman pay for my food.
I had all of $15 with me, which a friend of mine gave me.
It was on black market.
He gave me $15, when I went to the United States.
So I had some of the $15 with me.
But I paid for the lunch.
And this girl, the other student, was put up with some people.
And I was being taken to-- she told me, I'm taking you to a house, where some refugees live.
And you going to be put up with a family of refugees.
You're going to stay with them.
And I was brought to a house.
And when a woman opened the door-- this was somebody that I knew very well from Europe.
Not because she was from my hometown, which she was.
But because after the liberation, she lived in the same town where my father lived, and I knew her husband.
And so I was boarding with her.
I gained 20 pounds in four weeks.
That's how well she took care of me.
And I started looking for a job.
The first job I went-- when I came to Atlanta, they had a bus strike.
So I had walked Atlanta far and wide, looking for a job on foot.
The first job I applied for was in the At that time, they had I wanted a job at a newspaper.
Coming over on the boat, I edited a newspaper on the boat.
Everybody had to work.
I wrote for a newspaper in the University of Frankfurt.
I sent some articles to I felt that I would like to go into that particular field.
And this head of personnel took me aside and he said, look, you're not an American, you're not a Southerner, and you're Jewish.
You have three strikes on you before you start.
If I get you a job, you're going to be stuck in the copy room for the rest of your days.
Do you have any other skills?
I said, yes.
I'm a machinist.
I worked in the machine shop.
I went to vocational schools.
He said, get yourself a job in the machine shop.
So I got a job in the American Machine and Foundry.
It was owned by a Jewish man.
And I worked there close to a year.
In the interim, I applied for my first papers.
When the shop closed-- the man went out of business or sold it out to somebody, I don't remember-- I got a job with Marietta Aircraft Factory.
And Monday morning, I arrived with my toolbox, ready to work.
You had to fill out a little paper.
And there was a question, are you a citizen or not?
And of course I wasn't a citizen.
He says, we have to get you clearance.
I says, clearance from whom?
He says, from the FBI.
I says, how long does it take?
About six months.
Well, I says, I can't wait six months because I have to eat.
So let me go to the FBI and get my own clearance.
I went to the FBI.
I think they were on Peachtree and Sixth or Seventh Street at that time.
And I asked for somebody who was in charge of clearance, and a young man came out.
I says, can you give me clearance?
I'm applying for a job, a defense job.
He says, sure and brings me.
I says, how long will it take?
He says, six months.
I says, I don't need you for that.
I says, I need clearance now.
I want to start working.
Well, in the meanwhile, I filled out the forms.
He says, I can't help you.
And so I left Atlanta.
And I came up north.
I came to the Bronx.
A friend of mine lived in the Bronx.
And eventually I found a job in New Jersey.
I met my wife to my second cousin, who was my wife's best girlfriend in December of 1952.
In January, 1955 we were married.
I was working for Thomas Edison in West Orange as a machinist.
I got laid off about the time my wife was pregnant.
And I made the decision that I cannot depend on other people's decisions.
And I went into my own-- started my own machine shop, while I was working for somebody else.
And I accomplished this by working 36 hours in a row, sleeping eight hours, for a little over a year until I completed enough savings to buy some more equipment.
And eventually, I went into a partnership with somebody who was in the camp was me.
And the machine shop was in Brooklyn and then eventually moved into Jamaica.
And I came-- in 1968, I moved in from New Jersey to West Hempstead, where I'm presently residing with my beautiful wife.
My children-- we have two sons.
The older one is 37, he's an attorney.
He's married, he lives in Santa Fe, he's got a year and a half old son.
He's very much involved in pro bono work.
He works for the Native Americans without pay, represents them.
My younger son graduated Union College as a political science major.
He spent one year as an intern in Albany, one year in England as an aide to a member of Parliament.
He was responsible for re-election of this member of Parliament.
He instituted American campaigning methods.
One year he spent in Washington, working fro a Congressman.
And his senior year, he wrote his thesis and he graduated from Union College.
And he was selected from among many applicants to be one of the 48 Coro candidates.
The Coro Foundation is an organization that trains people for work in public service.
Many of our senators and dignitaries are graduates of it.
Senator Feingold-- President Bush and Mr.
Buchanan were turned down.
They were not qualified to get into that particular program.
By the way, Mrs.
Bush was honored, I think, by the Coro Foundation, about three years ago.
He spent one year in St.
Louis as a trainee.

And then he volunteered for one year of working for the organization in Los Angeles, where he created a program for high school children, from mostly Chicano sections, where he took 12 children every three months, and he trained them.
What he did, he taught them how to shed the prejudices, perceptions.
He exposed them to successful minorities.
He installed in them faith in hope, in life.
Many of them, he had many letters.
There's some paralegals from among his, paramedics, the people he trained.
He spent one year over there.
Then he came--
Today he's in Pittsburgh.
He just took a job with Ernst Young as a consultant management.
Because later on, he went to Washington and worked for an environmental firm.
He was the youngest person on the Coro Board, and the youngest director of training in New York City, where he trained for one year.
And then he went to Atlanta.
Because he got married over there, and his wife went to medical school.
She graduated and she's now doing her residence in Pittsburgh.
So David went to Pittsburgh and took a job over there.
My father, in 1948, when the war in Israel broke out, the war of independence, he volunteered to go to the Israeli Army.
But he got as far as Marseilles, when somebody discovered that he was 10 years older than he declared himself.
So he couldn't go.
So he stayed in Paris.
I visited him in 1949 in Paris before I came to the United States.
In 1952, my father remarried in Paris, a wonderful woman with two children.
In 1956, I was able to bring my father to the United States with his wife and his stepdaughter.
My father passed away in 1980.
If I just can tell you, just in very few words, a short story.
I told you about [GERMAN] Schwammberger, when he came to our camp, when it became known what kind of person he was, we named him the angel of death, the Malach Hamavet.
When my father was dying, about two or three days before he died, I went to see him in Irvington.
He was in the Irvington General, in the smaller hospital there.
And I found him tied down to the bed and unconscious.
And I asked the resident why he was doing this.
So he said my father was hallucinating in strange languages.
And for his own safety, they tied him down.
And I told them never, ever to do it again.
And when my father regained, consciousness, I asked him, Pa, what happened?
He spoke to me in Yiddish.
He says, the Malach Hamavet, the angel of death, was here.
And I thought he was speaking about what people-- before they expire, they worry about the angel of death.
I says, Pa, the war, you're not dying.
You're fine, you'll be all right.
He says, no, no.
You don't understand.
He didn't remember the name.
It was the SS angel of death.
He stood in the door with his gun facing me.
He wants to take me.
Shortly after that, Mr.
Schwammberger was apprehended in Argentina and after several years, extradited to Germany.
And he was tried.
While he was tried in Germany, there was a hue and cry, why are we doing this?
Why are we renewing the old wounds?
And it's an old story, it's not necessary.
It has no relevance, and there's no point in it.
And all I could think about my father's dying and not knowing whether he passed through the other world with a picture of this man.
To me, it was relevant.
The people that became friends in the camp created a bond of friendship that is stronger than anything, than any other bond.
Maybe only soldiers who face the moments of truth together can understand it.
I have friends all over the world-- in Argentina and Australia and Israel, some of them still in Germany.
My children consider my friends their extended family.
There's no hesitation from my children to go and stay with my friends.
Because they are received like a member of the family.
The same way goes for the children of my friends.
My son went to Australia, he stayed for six weeks with my friend over there.
The bonds are unbroken.
When we meet each other, we do not share experiences.
Sometimes just a look at each other's face or a word or a glance exchanged tells more in one second than volumes could tell.
I mean, this fellow who is in Australia is my friend Milek Salpeter, who lost three toes in the camp in Leitmeritz.
He knows that I gave my last ration of food.
He knows that.
When he came first time to the United States, he asked me to immigrate to Australia.
He was very successful, he was a builder.
He was very wealthy.
He says, you go to Australia with me, be my partner.
Everything I own, half-- [SOBS] half of it is yours.
That's the extent of the bond that we have created.
There is an organization that is in existence for 30 years, called the New Krakow Friendship Society.
The membership consists of survivors from Krakow and vicinity.
Some people are from other-- if their spouses are from Krakow, they may be from someplace else.
But most of-- The raison d'etre for this organization's existence is charity, mainly.
What we do, we-- I am to be the welfare chairman.
We send money every month to Jews in Krakow, to elderly Jews.
Unfortunately, their numbers are dwindling.
From 70-some charges, we have now about 50 left.
We support Jews in Israel and also needy survivors from our organizations in New York.
And this is all done by charitable endeavors, by fundraisings.
And besides that, we contribute to many other charitable organizations.
We gave close to half a million dollars to Beit Halochem, which is an organization that takes care of Israeli disabled veterans.
So at our world gathering in Florida about seven years ago, we committed ourselves.
And we collected close to half a million dollars and we gave to this organization.
Because we felt-- they put a plaque in the name of Krakow Jewry-- we felt this was the best way to honor [SOBS] our loved ones.
We felt that this was the best way to pass on the legacy of their values, of their ethical will, so to speak, the things that we're exposed to, that we cherish most.
Most of these values, really, I wasn't aware of them.
As I told you, I took philosophy, and I took Nietzsche, Kant, and all the other philosophers.
And I came away very cold, very disappointed.
And I turned to the treasury of the Jewish people.
And I started to study the things that I should have studied when I was young.
And I started to appreciate the philosophy of our faith and the values that our faith teaches us.
And those were the values.
The people, even if they were not educated in Jewish law, in Jewish traditions, they knew.

I lived under three guidelines, the three words that guided of my life from my father-- [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-GERMAN], which means, you are forbidden, it's not done, and it's not becoming a Jew.
You knew that a Jew doesn't go drunk in the street.
A Jew takes care of another Jew.

Make sure that you help, that you give the ones that don't have, even if you have less, that you do not hurt other person's feeling, that you not embarrass another person, that you show respect to authority, that you show respect for elders.
All these things-- they were not taught in the way that the Torah is taught.
But they were taught as a way of life.
This was a way of life.
You don't do that.
You know the law.
And if you ask why, he says, don't ask.
The way of teaching in the [INAUDIBLE] in Europe was not the way the young people would expect--
